title: An Algorithm for the Machine Calculation of Complex Fourier Series
summary: Introduced the radix-2 divide-and-conquer FFT formulation that reduced DFT complexity from quadratic to near-linearithmic and enabled practical spectral computing.
assumptions: Input length can be recursively factored (especially powers of two).
Complex arithmetic cost model is dominant in runtime analysis.
contributions: Formalized recursive decomposition of DFT into smaller DFTs.
Established asymptotic speedup foundation for modern FFT software/hardware.
claims: Recursive factorization drastically reduces arithmetic operation count versus direct DFT.
conclusions: FFT is a practical algorithmic primitive for large transform workloads.
limitations: Operation-count analysis does not model modern cache/memory hierarchy effects.
future_work: Algorithm variants for mixed radices and architecture-aware implementations.
evidence: DOI landing page and canonical citation metadata.
bibtex: @article{cooley1965algorithm,title={An Algorithm for the Machine Calculation of Complex Fourier Series},author={Cooley, James W. and Tukey, John W.},journal={Mathematics of Computation},volume={19},number={90},pages={297--301},year={1965},doi={10.1090/S0025-5718-1965-0178586-1}}

title: FFTW: An Adaptive Software Architecture for the FFT
summary: Presented FFTW planning and runtime adaptation, showing superior practical performance over many fixed implementations.
assumptions: Planner overhead can be amortized across repeated transform calls.
Microarchitecture-specific tuning materially impacts runtime.
contributions: Introduced codelets and planning search over decomposition trees.
Demonstrated architecture-adaptive performance gains.
claims: Self-optimizing planning usually outperforms generic/public FFT implementations.
conclusions: Empirical tuning is essential for FFT performance portability.
limitations: Planning time can be high for one-off transforms.
future_work: Broader SIMD and multicore-aware planner strategies.
evidence: DOI metadata and FFTW project page references the ICASSP paper.
bibtex: @inproceedings{frigo1998fftw,title={FFTW: An adaptive software architecture for the FFT},author={Frigo, Matteo and Johnson, Steven G.},booktitle={Proceedings of ICASSP},pages={1381--1384},year={1998},doi={10.1109/ICASSP.1998.681704}}

title: The Design and Implementation of FFTW3
summary: Detailed FFTW3 architecture including planner search space, code generation, and portability techniques for high performance.
assumptions: Empirical plan search approximates optimal implementation choice.
Composite-size decomposition dominates real workloads.
contributions: Systematized planner heuristics and codelet generation.
Documented reproducible benchmarking methodology for FFT libraries.
claims: Planner-driven approach delivers robust cross-platform performance.
conclusions: Autotuned FFT libraries can remain competitive across architecture generations.
limitations: Planning and codelet generation complexity can limit constrained deployments.
future_work: More aggressive vectorization and multicore/cluster extensions.
evidence: Canonical DOI link and FFTW homepage citations.

title: SPIRAL: A Generator for Platform-Adapted Libraries of Signal Processing Algorithms
summary: Introduced a DSL and search-based synthesis system for architecture-adapted FFT and related transform kernels.
assumptions: Transform identities can be represented compositionally and searched automatically.
Performance objective can be approximated from generated kernel timings.
contributions: Transform formula language + code generation pipeline.
Automatic search over algorithmic and implementation variants.
claims: Generated code can match or exceed expert-tuned libraries on target platforms.
conclusions: Program generation is viable for FFT portability and sustained optimization.
limitations: Search cost grows rapidly with transform family and hardware options.
future_work: Heterogeneous targets and richer performance models.
evidence: Full publication metadata and DOI available from university index pages.

summary: Presented scalable 3D FFT with pencil decomposition and reported strong/weak scaling on large HPC systems.
assumptions: Distributed-memory transpose dominates scaling at high process counts.
Pencil decomposition alleviates slab decomposition limits.
contributions: Portable framework for large-scale 3D FFT.
Practical guidance on decomposition and parameter choices.
claims: 2D decomposition improves scalability compared with slab methods.
conclusions: Pencil decomposition is preferable for extreme-scale 3D FFT workloads.
limitations: Performance still communication-bound at very large scale.
future_work: Hybrid MPI+threads/GPU extensions.

summary: Analyzed 3D FFT communication lower bounds and architecture implications for exascale systems.
assumptions: Bulk-synchronous communication model with latency/bandwidth terms.
Global transpose cost dominates at scale.
contributions: Communication-centric model for parallel 3D FFT.
Implications for decomposition and network-aware design.
claims: Communication, not flops, is the limiting factor at extreme scale.
conclusions: Algorithmic choices must target communication reduction first.
limitations: Model calibration depends on system/network specifics.
future_work: Topology-aware communication schedules and overlap strategies.
evidence: DOI and citation metadata from linked repositories/indices.

summary: Compared collective communication designs for large distributed FFT on multi-GPU systems and motivated heFFTe design choices.
assumptions: MPI collectives govern distributed transpose performance.
GPU-CPU and network paths materially affect total FFT runtime.
contributions: Empirical analysis on Summit for communication bottlenecks.
Design recommendations for GPU-centric distributed FFT.
claims: Collective strategy strongly affects large-scale FFT throughput.
conclusions: Communication-aware library design is mandatory for exascale FFT.

title: heFFTe: Highly Efficient FFT for Exascale
summary: Introduced heFFTe for exascale-targeted distributed FFT with strong GPU scalability and communication models.
key_equations: N_0/P_{i0}\times N_1/P_{i1}\times N_2/P_{i2}\xrightarrow[]{reshape+1D\ FFTs}\hat N_0/P_{o0}\times\hat N_1/P_{o1}\times\hat N_2/P_{o2}
assumptions: 3D FFT can be decomposed into staged 1D FFT + transposes.
Communication dominates at high node counts.
contributions: Portable C++/CUDA+MPI library for large-scale FFT.
Detailed algorithmic decomposition and communication analysis.
claims: Reported >40x local-kernel gains vs CPU baselines and >2x full FFT gains in tested settings.
conclusions: heFFTe is suitable as a core exascale FFT component.
limitations: Performance remains sensitive to network collective behavior.
future_work: More backends and deeper communication/computation overlap.
evidence: Full-text PDF scraped from ICL page includes algorithm and communication model.

title: 3D DFT by Block Tensor-Matrix Multiplication via a Modified Cannon's Algorithm: Implementation and Scaling on Distributed-Memory Clusters with Fat Tree Networks
venue: Journal of Parallel and Distributed Computing
summary: Presents S3DFT, an alternative distributed 3D DFT method that reduces all-to-all usage by employing point-to-point communication with modified Cannon-style tensor-matrix multiplication.
assumptions: Communication overhead dominates strong-scaling behavior in standard 3D FFT pipelines.
Point-to-point exchange patterns can outperform all-to-all on some fat-tree deployments despite higher arithmetic cost.
contributions: Introduces S3DFT distributed algorithm and implementation.
Provides scaling analysis on fat-tree networked clusters and contrasts communication behavior with parallel FFT.
claims: Alternative communication pattern can improve scalability in selected network regimes.
Communication characteristics can outweigh extra arithmetic complexity.
conclusions: Communication-aware algorithm selection is environment-dependent; FFT is not universally optimal at scale.
limitations: Comparisons are tied to tested cluster/network topologies and implementation choices.
future_work: Broader topology studies and hybrid strategy selection between FFT and tensor-multiplication DFT.
evidence: Search scrape exposes DOI 10.1016/j.jpdc.2024.104945 and abstract highlights on communication bottlenecks.

title: GPU-enabled Extreme-Scale Turbulence Simulations: Fourier Pseudo-Spectral Algorithms at the Exascale using OpenMP Offloading
summary: Reports exascale Fourier pseudo-spectral turbulence simulations on Frontier and characterizes performance constraints and scaling for FFT-intensive workloads.
assumptions: Pseudo-spectral Navier-Stokes solvers are FFT-bound at extreme resolutions.
OpenMP target offloading plus GPU kernels can sustain exascale throughput with careful memory/communication design.
contributions: Demonstrates production-scale FFT-heavy simulation up to tens of trillions of grid points.
Documents practical systems choices for exascale GPU pseudo-spectral pipelines.
claims: Frontier-scale runs show feasible extreme-resolution turbulence with GPU-first Fourier methods.
conclusions: FFT-centric pseudo-spectral algorithms remain viable at exascale with architecture-aware implementation.
limitations: Results are tied to Frontier hardware/software stack and turbulence workload specifics.
future_work: Portability evaluation across additional exascale systems and communication/runtime tuning layers.
evidence: OSTI record includes DOI 10.1016/j.cpc.2024.109364 and abstract-scale highlights.

title: Optimized Power and Speed of Split-Radix, Radix-4 and Radix-2 FFT Structures
venue: EURASIP Journal on Advances in Signal Processing
summary: Compares radix-2/radix-4/split-radix hardware implementations using a proposed parallel-prefix adder to reduce power-delay and improve speed.
assumptions: Adder architecture is a dominant factor in FPGA FFT energy-delay outcomes.
Split-radix decomposition can improve resource/performance balance versus radix-2 and radix-4 in tested hardware flow.
contributions: Introduces and evaluates a new parallel-prefix adder in multiple FFT structures.
Provides comparative power, area, and frequency results across radix variants.
claims: Split-radix configuration with proposed adder provides favorable speed-power-area tradeoff in tested setup.
conclusions: Hardware-level arithmetic design materially changes FFT implementation ranking.
limitations: Findings depend on selected FPGA toolchain/process and implementation settings.
future_work: ASIC evaluations and broader precision/size exploration.
evidence: Springer full-text scrape includes equations, abstract, and method sections with DOI metadata.

title: Design and Optimization of High-Performance Multi-Dimensional FFT Based on Matrix Core
venue: Journal of South China University of Technology (Natural Science Edition)
summary: Proposes matrix-core-based Stockham FFT optimization on AMD MI250, reporting improved 1D/2D/3D performance over rocFFT and VkFFT in tested cases.
assumptions: Matrix-core intrinsic utilization and memory-traffic reduction are primary levers for multidimensional GPU FFT speedups.
Stockham formulation maps effectively to matrix-multiplication style kernels.
contributions: Introduces register-level elementwise multiplication scheduling and shared-memory reordering for FFT.
Benchmarks against rocFFT and VkFFT on AMD MI250.
claims: Reports average performance gains for 1D/2D/3D FFT and large 3D advantage versus compared libraries on tested platform.
conclusions: Matrix-core-oriented design can improve practical GPU FFT throughput for multidimensional workloads.
limitations: Evaluation is platform-specific and may not generalize across GPU architectures.
future_work: Cross-platform validation and automated tuning for matrix-core FFT kernels.
evidence: SciOpen scrape includes abstract, DOI, and quantitative comparison statements.

title: Research on High-Performance Fourier Transform Algorithms Based on the NPU
summary: Investigates Fourier-transform acceleration strategies tailored to NPU-oriented heterogeneous SoCs with energy constraints for wearable/edge contexts.
assumptions: Conventional FFT mappings are suboptimal on NPU dataflow constraints.
Heterogeneous CPU+NPU scheduling can improve energy-performance tradeoffs for transform preprocessing.
contributions: Adapts Fourier computation strategy to NPU execution characteristics.
Evaluates performance/efficiency implications in edge-oriented contexts.
claims: NPU-aware transform strategy improves practical throughput/efficiency over naive mappings in tested scenarios.
conclusions: Hardware-specific transform mapping is essential for edge SoC deployments.
limitations: Generalization beyond studied NPU stack and application mix is limited.
future_work: Broader NPU families and mixed-precision numerical fidelity analysis.

title: MFFT: A GPU Accelerated Highly Efficient Mixed-Precision Large-Scale FFT Framework
venue: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
summary: Presents mixed-precision large-scale distributed FFT with shared-exponent communication compression and two-phase normalization, showing strong scaling to 4096 GPUs.
assumptions: Communication dominates at scale and can be reduced with lower-precision exchange while preserving overall accuracy through normalization.
Mixed-precision strategy is acceptable for target application error tolerances.
contributions: Introduces shared-exponent communication compression for distributed FFT.
Combines kernel, memory, and message optimizations in a large-scale GPU framework.
claims: Reported faster execution than double-precision baseline and stronger performance than selected open-source baselines on tested systems.
conclusions: Mixed-precision communication is a practical lever for large-scale FFT efficiency.
limitations: Accuracy-speed tradeoff depends on workload spectra and tolerance requirements.
future_work: Adaptive precision control and broader architecture portability analysis.
evidence: Discovery pages provide DOI 10.1145/3605148 and abstract metrics (4096 GPUs, speedup/efficiency deltas).

title: Performance Analysis of Parallel FFT on Large Multi-GPU Systems
summary: Analyzes multi-GPU multidimensional FFT performance with bandwidth/communication models and tuning across Summit/Spock systems.
assumptions: All-to-all and point-to-point communication tuning strongly impacts end-to-end FFT scaling.
Model-guided tuning can improve application-level performance for FFT-heavy workloads.
contributions: Introduces a parameterized algorithm including grid shrinking and batched transforms.
Develops bandwidth-centric performance analysis on large multi-GPU systems.
claims: Careful tuning can achieve near-linear scaling regimes and notable acceleration for FFT-dependent applications.
conclusions: Communication-model-guided parameter tuning is necessary for practical large GPU FFT deployments.
limitations: Measured outcomes are tied to Summit/Spock-era hardware and software stacks.
future_work: Extending tuning strategies to new exascale GPU architectures and libraries.
